title: Cross-substrate consumption — derived state, invariants, and read surfaces resolve foreign endpoints through the registry
description: Complete the symmetric peer graph the 2026-05-31 identity spec envisions ('substrates are wholly separate peers… related only by an explicit edge a user files; references are symmetric') by making foreign endpoints CONSUMED, not only filed and certified. The v0.28.0 arc shipped foreign IDENTITY — structured EdgeEndpoint with substrate_id, the project-root registry with aliases, resolveRef active/foreign/dangling/unregistered classification with per-pass foreign-index caching, promote-item lineage — and stopped before consumption: every edge-consuming derivation join (gate/dependency satisfaction, derivedRollupComplete membership completeness, the status-consistency invariant, requires-edge satisfaction, joinBlocks edge mode, roadmap rollups, walk traversals) computes endpointKey (the bare refname, substrate_id discarded) and resolves against the ACTIVE substrate's byRefname index only, so valid foreign endpoints are silently dropped (gates derive false-ready), defaulted (rollups derive false-never-complete, and reconcile/converge stamp that status durably), skipped (invariants), qualifier-erased (walks), or collision-mis-resolved onto a same-refname local item — while validateContext's edge-integrity certifies the same edges as valid. This feature routes every such join through registry-aware endpoint resolution (resolveRef or an equivalent resolveEndpointLoc shim with the existing foreignCache), making cross-substrate edges carry derived consequence: a task gated on an open foreign item derives blocked with the substrate-qualified blocker; a milestone whose foreign member is complete in its home substrate can reach; declared invariants compare foreign targets per spec §J3 (compare-if-readable, warn-if-unreadable). The topologies this serves are the envisioned ones: transitional A/B cutover, per-arc scratch, frozen-substrate consultation, curated carry-forward, and concurrent operational peers with live cross-dependencies — the first use case demanding concurrent-peer derivation.
motivation: A downstream two-substrate project hit the gap on first real use: a task gated on an open foreign item derived ready and entered nextActions with validate clean. The upstream verification (analysis/2026-07-06-foreign-status-derivation-blindness.md) confirmed the mechanism at every derivation surface, demonstrated the refname-collision mis-resolution (a local closed same-refname item silently satisfied a foreign open gate — collisions are the EXPECTED case across catalog-installed substrates), and established the split-brain: the write path and integrity surface accept and certify foreign edges that derivation treats as nonexistent. The prior-art sweep found the consumption layer unfiled and undesigned (spec Phase J wired the resolver into validation only; §J3's foreign-aware status-consistency was designed, never implemented).
acceptance_criteria: Gate and dependency satisfaction (currentState incompletePreds/unsatisfiedGates) resolve foreign endpoints through the registry: a task gated on a foreign item that is not complete in its home substrate derives blocked, with the blocker carried substrate-qualified (alias:refname), and releases when the foreign item completes; the two-substrate reproduction fixture from the investigation, promoted to a regression test, passes.

derivedRollupComplete resolves foreign members through the registry — a rollup whose foreign member is complete in its home substrate can derive complete — and its three consumers (currentState gate satisfaction + milestones facet, the derived-status invariant, context-reconcile/converge-on-write) inherit the semantics through the shared helper with no per-consumer divergence.

The status-consistency invariant implements spec §J3 for foreign targets: the status comparison runs when the foreign item's block/status is readable, and an unreadable foreign substrate degrades LOUDLY (a warning naming the unresolvable endpoint), never to a silent skip; requires-edge foreign-satisfaction semantics are examined and recorded in the same decision, not left as fallout.

No derivation or read surface silently drops a foreign endpoint: joinBlocks edge mode, roadmap rollups, and the walk ops either resolve foreign counterparts or surface them explicitly with the substrate qualifier preserved in output; findReferences' structured-endpoint fidelity is pinned as the parity bar.

Refname-collision aliasing is eliminated: a foreign structured endpoint never key-collides onto an active-substrate item with the same refname, on any consuming surface (the normalizeEndpoint key qualifies foreignness or every consumer branches on the foreign flag); the collision reproduction from the investigation becomes a regression test asserting the foreign item's status is read, not the local impostor's.

The dangling-vs-foreign conflation in the gate guard is split: dangling targets keep their documented semantics, foreign targets follow the decided foreign-resolution semantics, and the two are distinguishable in diagnostics.

The foreign-resolution SEMANTICS — live cross-substrate status read vs derive-as-blocked-until-resolved vs flagged-exclusion, including freshness, cost, offline-substrate, and status-vocabulary-trust trade-offs — is recorded as a DECISION with its derivation shown before implementation lands; the implementation matches the decided semantics on every surface (no per-surface divergence).

Any vocabulary or invariant additions land dual-surface (live config + packaged catalog), and the feature's surfaces state the residue honestly: an unregistered or unreadable peer substrate degrades loudly to the decided fallback, never silently.
